rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE PORTSIDE 503 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Quash Subpoenas Duces Tecum
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. The Motion to Quash Subpoenas Duces Tecum is granted in part and denied in part. The Motion to Quash is granted as to the portions of the subpoenas seeking all documents and metadata of those documents for accounts held in the name of Decedent Jim Browne or Respondent. Petitioners have not met their burden to show that the interests of discovery disclosure outweigh the individuals' privacy rights (see Hinshaw Winkeler v. Sup. Ct. (1996) 51 Cal.App.4th 233, 237 and Fortunato v. Sup. Ct (2003) 114 Cal. App. 4th 475). This proceeding is now focused on whether a trust exists and, if so, what are the trust assets. Petitioners have not demonstrated that information on accounts held in decedent's or respondent's names is probative on the relevant issues or likely to lead to the discovery of relevant evidence. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 2017.010.) On the other hand, the privacy interest weighing against discovery disclosure is substantial. Respondent did not waive her privacy interests when she listed the subpoenaed accounts as community property in the Spousal Property Petition filed in the related case (PES-18-302335). The relevance being minimal and the privacy interests substantial, the privacy interests predominate. As to the subpoenaed accounts held in the name of the Portside 503 Trust, the Motion is denied as to all documents related to subpoenaed accounts held in the name of the Trust, except that the motion is granted as to the metadata of those documents. Respondent's argument that Petitioners lack standing to seek Trust accountings covering the period of time when the Trust was revocable is irrelevant to the discovery issues before the court. Information regarding accounts held in the name of Portside 503 Trust is highly relevant to the issues of whether such a trust exists and the identity of trust assets. Petitioners, however, have not shown that the metadata is relevant to the pending action or is otherwise reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 2017.010.) Petitioners' procedural objections to the motion are overruled. The errors are excusable and, in any event, Petitioners have not demonstrated prejudice and no substantial prejudice is apparent on this record. Going forward, however, the parties will be expected to satisfy all requirements for discovery motions. The parties' competing requests for sanctions are denied. Respondent shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on August 14, 2019.
